did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used 'Model Dermatology', a publicly testable neural network (ensemble of CNNs/ResNet variants) to classify 184 skin disorders and determine malignancy in dermatology datasets (RD, SNU, Edinburgh, TeleDerm). The AI algorithm's performance was evaluated and compared against dermatologists and laypersons.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The study presents original experimental results evaluating the performance of the 'Model Dermatology' neural network algorithm on four distinct datasets (RD, SNU, Edinburgh, TeleDerm). It includes a retrospective analysis of patient-driven internet images, compares algorithm performance against dermatologists and laypersons, and reports specific metrics (accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity) with statistical analysis, indicating it is an original research article rather than a review.

title: The degradation of performance of a state‑of‑the‑art skin image classifier when applied to patient‑driven internet search
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of 'Model Dermatology,' a state-of-the-art ensemble of CNNs classifying 184 skin disorders, on patient-driven internet images compared to curated clinical datasets. The algorithm was tested on four datasets: the Reddit (RD) internet community dataset, the SNU and Edinburgh tertiary care datasets, and the TeleDerm dataset. While the AI achieved dermatologist-level performance on curated clinical datasets (SNU and Edinburgh), its accuracy significantly deteriorated on the RD and TeleDerm datasets due to insufficient image quality and out-of-distribution disorders, respectively. Specifically, the algorithm's Top-1 accuracy on the RD dataset was 39.2%, which was equivalent to general physicians but superior to laypersons. The results demonstrate that AI algorithms performing at dermatologist levels in controlled, in-distribution settings may fail to maintain this performance in real-world, out-of-distribution settings characterized by variable image quality and unseen pathologies.

lesion_types_studied.details: The Model Dermatology algorithm was trained to classify 184 specific skin disorders. The study evaluates performance across these classes in multi-class classification tasks.

Used in binary classification for determining malignancy. Counts: RD dataset (123 cases, 9.6%), SNU dataset (182 cases, 8.7%), Edinburgh dataset (481 cases, 37.0%), TeleDerm dataset (4 cases, 1.2%). Ground truth determined by pathological examinations in SNU, Edinburgh, and TeleDerm datasets.

Used in binary classification for determining malignancy (as the non-malignant counterpart). Counts: RD dataset (896 cases, 69.9%), SNU dataset (620 cases, 29.5%), Edinburgh dataset (819 cases, 63.0%), TeleDerm dataset (28 cases, 8.2%). Ground truth for RD dataset voted on by five specialists.

all_task_types.target: 184 skin disorders (multi-class) and malignancy status (binary: malignant vs. benign)
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates 'Model Dermatology', an ensemble of CNNs, for multi-class classification of 184 skin disorders (measured by Top-1/Top-3 accuracy) and binary classification for determining malignancy (measured by AUC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV). The primary task is classification of discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: RD dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of patient-driven internet images collected from the Reddit melanoma community (r/melanoma) between January 25, 2020, and July 30, 2021. Images were posted by laypersons. Ground truth was determined by consensus of five specialists (four dermatologists and one plastic surgeon).

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on out-of-distribution, patient-captured images with variable quality.
use.other: Used for reader study comparison with laypersons and general physicians.

datasets_used.name: SNU dataset
datasets_used.description: A tertiary care center dataset comprising clinical photographs from Seoul National University Hospital, Inje University Hospital, and Hallym University Hospital. Ground truth for malignancies was determined by pathological examinations.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on curated clinical images from tertiary care centers.
use.other: A subset of 240 images was publicly available for external testing.

datasets_used.name: Edinburgh dataset
datasets_used.description: A commercially available dataset (Dermofit Image Library) from a tertiary care center. Ground truth for malignancies was determined by pathological examinations.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on curated clinical images from tertiary care centers.

datasets_used.name: TeleDerm dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of consecutive clinical photographs captured in patients at Pontificia Universidad Católica in Chile between March 27 and April 30, 2020. Ground truth was mostly determined by clinical impression.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on teledermatology images, noting the presence of out-of-distribution disorders.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Calculating statistical difference between AUC values for determining malignancy across different datasets.
Comparing sensitivities, specificities, and Top accuracies after Shapiro test for normality.
Comparing Top-1/3 accuracies between adequate and inadequate image quality subsets in the RD dataset.
Comparing algorithm's Top-1 and Top-3 accuracies against general physicians' performance in the RD dataset.
Checking normality of data before applying t-tests.
Generating 95% confidence intervals for all samples.
Determining statistical significance in all analyses.

hypothesis_testing.test: DeLong method, Two-tailed t-test (including one-sample t-test), Chi-squared test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the compared metrics (e.g., AUC values between datasets, algorithm accuracy vs. general physicians' accuracy, accuracy between adequate and inadequate image quality subsets).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the compared metrics.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using a threshold of P < 0.05. The DeLong method was used to compare AUC values for malignancy determination, revealing significant differences between the RD dataset and the SNU/Edinburgh datasets (P < 0.0001). One-sample t-tests were used to compare algorithm Top-1/3 accuracies against general physicians, finding no significant difference for Top-1 (P = 0.25) but a significant difference for Top-3 (P = 0.014). Chi-squared tests showed significant differences in algorithm accuracy based on image quality (P = 0.0003 for Top-1, P = 0.0001 for Top-3).

RD dataset.description: Internet community dataset (1,282 images). Ground truth by consensus of five specialists.
RD dataset.notes: Algorithm performance significantly affected by image quality (P=0.0003 for Top-1, P=0.0001 for Top-3). Top-1 accuracy equivalent to general physicians (P=0.25), but Top-3 accuracy superior (P=0.014).

SNU dataset.description: Tertiary care center dataset (2,101 images total, 2,201 in methods text/Table 2, 240 public subset). Ground truth by pathological examination.
SNU dataset.notes: Performance equivalent to dermatologists in curated clinical datasets. AUC significantly higher than RD dataset (P < 0.0001).

Edinburgh dataset.description: Commercially available dataset (Dermofit Image Library) from tertiary care center (1,300 images). Ground truth by pathological examination.
Edinburgh dataset.notes: Performance equivalent to dermatologists in curated clinical datasets. AUC significantly higher than RD dataset (P < 0.0001).

TeleDerm dataset.description: Teledermatology dataset from Chile (340 consecutive images). Ground truth mostly by clinical impression.

binary_classification_malignancy.value: null (Excluded from malignancy analysis due to only 4 malignancies)

TeleDerm dataset.notes: Performance deteriorated due to out-of-distribution disorders (10.3% of images belonged to OOD classes). Accuracy inferior to dermatologists.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a generated dataset of 49,567 images; data-centric approach involving crawling the internet to collect and annotate images of common disorders (e.g., contact dermatitis) and improving diagnostic accuracy for subjects of various ethnicities. Classes were adjusted by removing unnecessary classes (e.g., graft-versus-host-disease) and adding common conditions (e.g., erosion) to reach 184 disease classes.

architecture_details.specific_components: Region-based CNN (RCNN) used to detect various shapes of both lesional and non-lesional areas to generate the training dataset and improve diagnosis of minor lesions.

training_details.training_strategy: Data-centric approach; annotation of huge number of image crops; reinforcement by crawling internet for high-prevalence disorders; removal of unnecessary classes; addition of common condition classes.

classification_or_output_head.type: Multi-class classification and binary malignancy scoring
classification_or_output_head.details: Predicts 184 skin conditions. Malignancy score calculated as sum of malignant outputs plus 0.2 times premalignant outputs. High sensitivity threshold defined as 90% sensitivity; high specificity threshold defined as 80% sensitivity.

preprocessing_techniques: Image quality assessment using a fine image selector submodule to classify images as 'adequate' (submodule output > 0.1) or 'inadequate'
Exclusion of postoperative photographs (26 images), dermoscopy images (6 images), intraocular lesion photographs (2 images), and non-clinical photographs (16 images) from the RD dataset
Exclusion of cases with inadequate image quality (mostly blurry) or lack of metadata (24 images) from the RD dataset
Combination of lichen amyloidosis and amyloidosis classes in the SNU dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Training data was reinforced by crawling the Internet to collect and annotate images of common disorders (e.g., contact dermatitis) with high prevalence rates to improve diagnostic accuracy in subjects of various ethnicities.
A region-based CNN was used to detect various shapes of both lesional and non-lesional areas to generate the training dataset, specifically to improve the diagnosis of minor lesions.
Unnecessary classes (e.g., graft-versus-host-disease) were removed, and classes corresponding to common conditions (e.g., erosion) were added to reach 184 disease classes for training.

A fine image selector submodule was used to classify images as 'adequate' (submodule output > 0.1) or 'inadequate'. Images with inadequate quality (mostly blurry) or lack of metadata were excluded from the RD dataset during construction.
Postoperative photographs (26 images), dermoscopy images (6 images), intraocular lesion photographs (2 images), and non-clinical photographs (16 images) were excluded from the RD dataset.

conclusions: The 'Model Dermatology' algorithm achieved dermatologist-level performance on curated clinical datasets from tertiary care centers (SNU and Edinburgh).
The algorithm's performance significantly deteriorated when applied to patient-driven internet images (RD dataset) due to insufficient image quality.
The algorithm's performance significantly deteriorated when applied to teledermatology images (TeleDerm dataset) due to the presence of out-of-distribution disorders.
On the RD dataset, the algorithm's Top-1 accuracy (39.2%) was equivalent to that of general physicians (36.8%) and superior to laypersons (19.2%).
Algorithm performance was significantly affected by image quality, with Top-1 accuracy dropping from 43.2% for adequate images to 32.9% for inadequate images.

AI algorithms that perform at dermatologist levels in controlled, in-distribution settings may not maintain this performance in real-world, out-of-distribution settings characterized by variable image quality and unseen pathologies.
The study highlights that algorithm performance is vulnerable to changes in intended settings, such as the shift from specialist-curated images to patient-captured images.
Laypersons' diagnostic accuracy did not deteriorate with inadequate image quality, whereas the algorithm's performance did, suggesting different reliance on visual cues.
important_remarks: The algorithm's performance significantly deteriorated in real-world settings compared to curated clinical datasets: Top-1 accuracy dropped from 54.5-64.0% (tertiary care) to 39.2% (RD) and 46.5% (TeleDerm).
Performance degradation in the RD dataset was significantly driven by insufficient image quality; Top-1 accuracy was 43.2% for adequate images versus 32.9% for inadequate images (P=0.0003).
Performance degradation in the TeleDerm dataset was significantly driven by out-of-distribution (OOD) disorders, with 10.3% of images belonging to OOD classes.
The algorithm's performance in the RD dataset was equivalent to general physicians (Top-1: 39.2% vs 36.8%, P=0.25) but superior to laypersons (Top-1: 19.2%).

Unlike the algorithm, human readers' (general physicians) performance did not significantly deteriorate with inadequate image quality (Top-1: 35.8% vs 38.4%, P>0.05), suggesting different reliance on visual cues.
The study highlights a critical limitation: algorithms trained on tertiary care data may fail in real-world settings due to distribution shifts (image quality and unseen pathologies).

The RD dataset ground truth was determined by consensus of five specialists, whereas malignancies in SNU, Edinburgh, and TeleDerm were determined by pathological examination (except TeleDerm where it was mostly clinical impression).
The algorithm's performance was not assessed in detail for darker skin populations, representing a potential bias and limitation for generalizability.

The study is retrospective; the authors note that few prospective studies or randomized controlled trials exist for deep learning tools in dermatology, casting doubt on the predictive value of retrospective results for real-world performance.
The TeleDerm dataset had a very low prevalence of malignancy (1.2%, n=4), leading to its exclusion from the binary malignancy analysis (AUC not reported).
